topic: Post-Hoc Retrodiction — prediction vs retrodiction distinction
our_argument_summary: Section 4.6.3 identifies that 52 of 64 reviewed WINs adopt published observations as 'predictions' after the fact. It distinguishes prediction (value stated before measurement) from retrodiction (value stated after measurement is known). It notes that the dome's timestamp infrastructure (git commits, OpenTimestamps) records when the webpage was committed, not when the derivation was first performed. It further notes that only 4 of 64 reviewed WINs derive their predicted values from dome geometry — the rest adopt values from standard physics or observational databases. The section concludes by synthesizing the three Part 4.6 patterns (monitoring illusion, relabeling, retrodiction) as a pipeline converting established knowledge into dome 'confirmed predictions' via label changes.

holes_found.description: The 12 WINs tagged post_hoc=false appear to be largely miscategorized, and NONE of the 12 tags include a post_hoc_evidence field explaining the reasoning. Examination of the 12 shows: WIN-003 (King's Chamber 117 Hz from Reid 1997 — measured decades before dome model), WIN-005 (African SAA cell from CHAOS-7 data — published before dome model), WIN-006 (NP linear drift from NOAA data covering 1590-1990 — published before dome model), WIN-009 (Telluric ~12 Hz from established MT literature), WIN-015 (Meyl's experiments from his prior publications), WIN-020 (lunar 18.6-yr cycle known since the 18th century), WIN-021 (gyroscopic precession from established physics), WIN-029 (Schumann 1952 derivation), WIN-054 (El Gordo discovered 2012, well before dome model). All of these involve observations published in the scientific literature before the dome model incorporated them. At minimum 10 of the 12 should be post_hoc=true, which would raise the count from 52/64 to approximately 62/64 (97%). The section's published count of 52/64 (81%) significantly UNDERSTATES the actual post-hoc problem. A dome defender wouldn't exploit this — it makes our argument stronger — but it undermines the credibility of the code_analysis infrastructure. If 10 tags are wrong in one direction, a defender can ask: 'How many of the 52 post_hoc=true tags are wrong in the OTHER direction?'
holes_found.recommendation: Audit all 12 post_hoc=false WINs. For each, add a post_hoc_evidence field documenting when the observation was first published vs. when the dome model first claimed it. The only plausible post_hoc=false candidates are: WIN-031 (theological claim with no measurable observation to be post-hoc about), WIN-044 (FSF is a dome-internal mathematical derivation used for future predictions), and possibly WIN-030 (elliptical geometry improvement, though it's fitting to existing data). Correct the remaining ~9-10 tags to post_hoc=true. This will automatically strengthen the section's dynamically-computed count.

holes_found.description: The section completely ignores the dome model's OWN prospective/backtested classification. The dome's wins.html page explicitly categorizes 9 WINs as 'PROSPECTIVE — Predicted Before Data' and implicitly labels the rest as 'BACKTESTED.' The predictions.html page defines: 'PROSPECTIVE: Timestamped before confirming data (Highest evidential weight — true advance prediction)' and 'BACKTESTED: Derived by fitting model to existing data (Valid but lower weight).' Our section argues that 52/64 WINs are post-hoc — but the dome itself admits that ~30 of 39 are backtested. A dome defender would say: 'You're attacking a strawman. We ALREADY distinguish prospective from backtested. We're transparent about this. The 9 prospective predictions are the ones with highest evidential weight.' By not engaging with the dome's own framework, our section looks like it's discovering what the dome openly acknowledges. This drastically weakens the rhetorical force. We need to: (1) acknowledge the dome's own classification, (2) explain why even the 9 'prospective' ones are problematic, and (3) show that the dome's transparency about backtesting doesn't salvage the overall predictive track record.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph directly addressing the dome's prospective/backtested distinction. Argue: (a) the 9 'prospective' predictions registered 2026-03-06 and confirmed 2026-03-12 are safe extrapolations of existing trends — registering that the SAA will continue declining for one more week is not a discriminating test; (b) the git timestamps prove when the WEBPAGE was updated, not when the dome's geometric equations first produced the value; (c) none of the 'prospective' WINs derive their predicted values from dome geometry — they set thresholds based on recent NOAA/INTERMAGNET trends and check them a week later; (d) even granting the dome full credit for 9 prospective predictions, 9 of 67 means 87% of claims are admittedly post-hoc, which is the section's central point.

holes_found.description: The 'derives_from_dome' count of 4/64 is presented as a separate point ('The derivation question') but is actually the CENTRAL issue that distinguishes meaningful retrodiction from trivial post-hoc adoption. The section buries the strongest argument in the third paragraph. If a post-hoc claim is derived from the model's own structure (geometric equations, parameters), it still has scientific value — as Newton's laws had value when they reproduced Kepler's already-known orbital data. The fact that only 4/64 WINs trace a derivation path from dome geometry to numerical prediction is the kill-shot: it means 60 of 64 claims are not just post-hoc but UNCONNECTED to the dome's own physics. This should be the lead argument, not a supporting detail.
holes_found.recommendation: Restructure to lead with the derivation question: 'Of 64 reviewed WINs, only 4 derive their predicted values from dome geometry. The remaining 60 state the already-known answer without connecting it to the model's equations. Retrodiction from first principles has scientific value — retrodiction by assertion does not.' Then use the 52/64 post-hoc count as supporting evidence: 'Furthermore, 52 of those 60 non-derived predictions adopt values that were published in the scientific literature before the dome model existed.' This framing is much harder for a dome defender to rebut.

holes_found.description: The section states 'only 4 actually derives its predicted value from the dome's own geometric equations' but doesn't name them. The 4 WINs are: WIN-020 (lunar 18.6-yr cycle via gears), WIN-044 (FSF from V12 geometry), WIN-057 (two-zone disc topology), WIN-058 (unified angular coordinate). A dome defender could examine these 4 and argue some are genuine geometric derivations, while a reviewer could note that even these 4 are questionable: WIN-020 reproduces a known period (18.6 years) using epicyclic gears fitted to the answer, WIN-044 derives a scaling function that hasn't yet been tested against future data (Aug 2026 eclipse pending), and WIN-057/058 are self-referential geometry definitions rather than empirical predictions. Not naming the 4 misses an opportunity to show that even the 'best case' examples are weak.
holes_found.recommendation: Name the 4 derives_from_dome=true WINs explicitly (or at least describe them generically). Note that even these represent fitted reproduction (WIN-020), untested future prediction (WIN-044), or tautological geometry definitions (WIN-057, 058) rather than genuine derivation-then-confirmation cycles.

holes_found.description: Section 4.6.3, like 4.6.1 and 4.6.2, is MISSING from build-doc-v4.js. The entire Part 4.6 code analysis subsections exist only in the HTML output. The downloadable DOCX and PDF — which some readers may treat as the authoritative version — contain none of the code analysis arguments. This was flagged in the SEC-4.6.1 and SEC-4.6.2 reviews as well. The gap is systematic, not incidental.
holes_found.recommendation: Add all Part 4.6 subsections (4.6.1, 4.6.2, 4.6.3) to build-doc-v4.js. This is a structural fix that should be done once for the entire Part 4.6, not piecemeal.

holes_found.description: The 'combined effect' paragraph makes a strong pipeline metaphor — 'a pipeline that converts established scientific knowledge into dome model confirmed predictions through a series of label changes.' But the metaphor overstates the uniformity. Not all 67 WINs exhibit all three patterns simultaneously. The unfalsifiable WINs (031, 032, 034) don't go through the 'pipeline' — they're theological assertions that never had a scientific observation to relabel. The self-contradicted WINs (002, 029, 038, etc.) DO derive predictions from dome geometry — they just get the wrong answer. Lumping everything into one pipeline narrative is rhetorically effective but imprecise. A dome defender could dissect individual exceptions to undermine the generalization.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a qualifier: 'Not every WIN follows this exact sequence — some are unfalsifiable claims with no testable content, others are self-contradicted when the dome's own geometry is applied — but the pipeline describes the dominant pattern across the majority of WINs.' This acknowledges the exceptions while preserving the point.

holes_found.description: The section's timestamp argument ('A prediction committed to git in 2025 about a phenomenon measured in 1952 is retrodiction, regardless of the commit date') is correct but doesn't engage with the dome's strongest counter: the 9 'prospective' predictions WERE registered before confirmation, per the dome's own framework. WIN-035-039 were registered 2026-03-06 and confirmed 2026-03-12. The git timestamps DO precede the confirming data for these specific WINs. Our blanket dismissal of timestamps doesn't hold for these cases. The stronger argument is that these are safe extrapolations of existing trends, not that the timestamps are meaningless.
holes_found.recommendation: Distinguish two cases: (1) For ~58 WINs, the timestamps are irrelevant because the observations precede the dome model by years to decades. (2) For the ~9 'prospective' WINs, the timestamps are valid but the predictions are trivial extrapolations. This two-pronged approach is much harder to rebut than a blanket timestamp dismissal.

holes_found.description: The section doesn't address the philosophical defense of retrodiction. A dome defender could cite the history of science: 'Newton's laws were first validated against Kepler's existing orbital data — that was retrodiction. Einstein's general relativity was first validated against Mercury's known perihelion precession — also retrodiction. Retrodiction IS how models are validated before they make novel predictions.' This is a legitimate philosophy-of-science argument. Our section dismisses retrodiction as inferior to prediction without addressing this standard defense. The key rebuttal is: Newton's retrodiction of Kepler's laws was mathematically derived from his gravitational equations (derives_from_dome=true, in our taxonomy), not just stated as matching. The dome model doesn't derive its retrodictions from its own equations for 60 of 64 WINs.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief acknowledgment: 'Retrodiction from first principles is valuable — Newton's gravitational laws reproducing Kepler's orbital data is a celebrated example. But Newton derived the orbital shapes from his equations, he did not simply assert that his model was consistent with Kepler. Of the 64 reviewed WINs, only 4 derive their values from dome equations; the remaining 60 are assertions of consistency without derivation.'

holes_found.description: The 'audit status' paragraph states the analysis covers '95% complete' (64 of 67). The 3 unreviewed WINs are WIN-032 (New Jerusalem pole axis, verdict: Unfalsifiable), WIN-033 (Sigma Octantis dimness, verdict: Refuted by Data), and WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess, verdict: Refuted by Data). WIN-032 is unfalsifiable and likely adds nothing to the post-hoc analysis. WIN-033 and WIN-065 are both about observational astronomy phenomena known long before the dome model — both would be post_hoc=true if reviewed. The 95% figure is technically correct but the missing 5% won't change the conclusions.
holes_found.recommendation: No prose change needed, but the remaining 3 WINs should be reviewed and tagged to reach 100% coverage. This is a tracker issue, not a section issue.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own prospective/backtested distinction is the most powerful evidence AGAINST it, not for it. The dome claims 9 of 39 WINs (23%) are prospective. By its own admission, 77% are backtested. Our section should lead with this: 'The dome model itself classifies only 9 of its 39 published WINs as prospective predictions — confirming that even by its own framework, 77% of its claimed successes are retrodictions. Our analysis shows the actual proportion is even higher (52 of 64, or 81%), because several WINs the dome calls prospective are actually safe extrapolations of existing trends.'

The dome's 'weekly test' framework (WIN-035 through WIN-039) can be analyzed quantitatively as a prediction discrimination test. Each weekly prediction sets a threshold based on recent data trends and checks one week later. For example, WIN-035 predicts 'SAA African cell < 21,795 nT' when the cell was already at ~21,850 nT and declining at ~30 nT/year. A one-week prediction of continued decline is essentially predicting that a moving object will continue moving. Calculate the base rate: given the SAA's observed rate of change, what's the probability that the weekly threshold would be crossed regardless of the model? If it's >90%, the 'prospective' label adds no discriminating power.

The 4 derives_from_dome=true WINs should be individually examined to test whether they represent genuine geometric derivation. WIN-020 (lunar 18.6-yr cycle) fits epicyclic gears to reproduce a period known since ancient astronomy — the gear ratios are reverse-engineered from the answer. WIN-044 (FSF) derives a scaling function from dome H(r)/r that is PENDING verification against the Aug 2026 eclipse — it's the one potentially genuine prospective derivation. WIN-057 and WIN-058 define coordinate systems and topological zones — these are definitions, not predictions. So the actual count of 'derived and empirically confirmed' predictions is closer to 1 (WIN-020, and even that is fitted), with 1 pending (WIN-044). This is far more damning than '4 derive from dome geometry.'

Cross-reference the post_hoc and derives_from_dome tags to build a 2x2 matrix. Of 64 reviewed WINs: (a) post_hoc=false AND derives_from_dome=true: 2 WINs (WIN-020, WIN-044) — these are the dome's best cases, potential genuine derivations. (b) post_hoc=true AND derives_from_dome=true: 2 WINs (WIN-057, WIN-058) — derived from dome geometry but about already-known phenomena. (c) post_hoc=true AND derives_from_dome=false: 50 WINs — the bulk, neither derived nor prospective. (d) post_hoc=false AND derives_from_dome=false: 10 WINs — not derived, and the post_hoc tags are questionable. This 2x2 matrix makes the argument visually clear: the dome's claimed 67 WINs contain at most 2 that are both prospective and geometrically derived.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model's author demonstrates awareness of the prediction vs retrodiction distinction and builds infrastructure around it. The prospective/backtested labeling, the git commit timestamps, and the OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring show genuine engagement with the epistemology of prediction. The 9 'prospective' WINs represent a real attempt to move beyond retrodiction toward genuine forecasting. The predictions.html page, with its registered thresholds and upcoming eclipse predictions, is the dome model's strongest methodological feature. Most alternative cosmology models make no predictions at all; the dome at least attempts to.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Awareness of the distinction between prediction and retrodiction does not guarantee the distinction is maintained in practice. The 9 'prospective' predictions are short-term extrapolations of existing trends (SAA continues declining, Schumann stays stable, North Pole continues drifting) — they demonstrate that the dome model can read current data and predict next week's measurement will be similar. This is meteorology, not theoretical physics. The August 2026 eclipse predictions (E001-E009) are the one case where dome geometry produces specific, falsifiable, discriminating predictions. If those succeed, the dome model has one genuine prospective geometric derivation. If they fail, the model's total score of genuine predictions drops to zero. Everything else — the 52+ post-hoc adoptions, the 41 relabelings, the 24 hardcoded monitoring checks — is infrastructure that creates the appearance of empirical science without the substance.

citations_unchecked: No external citations in this section — all claims reference internal code_analysis tags
citation_check.notes: This section contains no DOIs or external URLs. Its claims are all derived from the code_analysis tag counts in wins.json. The factual accuracy depends entirely on the accuracy of those tags — which, as noted in the critical holes above, appears to have significant errors in the post_hoc=false category.

deeper_analysis: Section 4.6.3 is the weakest of the three Part 4.6 subsections, not because its argument is wrong but because it doesn't engage with the dome model's own framework for addressing this exact issue. The dome model ALREADY acknowledges the prediction/retrodiction distinction and labels its WINs accordingly. By ignoring this, our section looks like it's making a discovery that the dome model openly concedes, which allows a defender to say 'we already told you that.' The section should instead pivot to: (1) even by the dome's own accounting, 77% are admittedly backtested; (2) our analysis shows the proportion is higher (81%+); (3) the 9 'prospective' WINs are trivial extrapolations; (4) the derivation question (only 4/64 derive from dome equations) is the real issue, and it applies equally to 'prospective' and 'backtested' WINs. Additionally, the post_hoc=false tags need urgent correction — at least 10 of 12 appear to be miscategorized, all lack evidence documentation, and if corrected the true count would be approximately 62/64 (97%). The section's current 52/64 figure actually understates the problem. Finally, the entire Part 4.6 (all three subsections) is absent from the DOCX/PDF output, which means the downloadable documents are missing the code analysis arguments entirely.
